output_text:
Critique of the OrchestrA abstract/intro, with concrete revision recommendations.

## Issues, in priority order

**1. The novelty claim is unverified and likely overstated.**
"To our knowledge, OrchestrA is the first orchestration substrate that combines role-typed message passing with workspace checkpointing for LLM agent teams." Both ingredients exist in prior systems (AutoGen, MetaGPT, CrewAI have role-typed routing; LangGraph and several agent frameworks support checkpointed state). The combination as the novelty hook is weak and easy to attack in review. Either (a) drop the "first" claim, (b) narrow it precisely (e.g., "first to expose content-addressed workspace rollback as a first-class scheduler primitive"), or (c) cite the closest prior systems and explain the delta.

**2. N=32 is too small to support the headline conclusion.**
"24 of 32 vs. 17 of 32" is a 7-task gap. Under a two-proportion test this is borderline; a McNemar test on paired tasks is the right statistic, and you don't report it. More importantly, the closing sentence — "orchestration substrate, rather than prompt engineering, is the binding constraint on multi-agent LLM team performance" — is a sweeping claim that 32 tasks cannot support. Either soften the claim ("on this benchmark, substrate-level changes outweighed the prompt-tuning we tried") or expand the evaluation. The current framing will draw a reject.

**3. The attribution argument has a confound.**
"We attribute the gap primarily to the workspace checkpointing layer: in 5 of 7 additional successes, the team rolled back at least once." Rollback occurring on a success path is correlational, not causal. The baseline is single-agent ReAct — it has no Reviewer either, so you cannot tell whether the gain is from checkpointing, from having a Reviewer at all, from role typing, or from the team structure. The proper ablation is OrchestrA-with-checkpointing vs. OrchestrA-without-checkpointing (same roles, same scheduler). Without that, the causal claim should be removed.

**4. Baseline is under-specified and self-serving.**
"A single-agent ReAct-style baseline that we re-implemented" — re-implemented baselines almost always underperform published ones. Name the reference implementation you tried to match, report whether your reimplementation reproduces its published numbers on any standard benchmark, and ideally compare against an off-the-shelf multi-agent baseline (AutoGen, MetaGPT) so the comparison isn't single-agent vs. multi-agent.

**5. Benchmark provenance is vague.**
"32 software-engineering tasks drawn from popular open-source repositories" — is this a subset of SWE-bench, SWE-bench Lite, a new benchmark, or hand-curated? If hand-curated, selection bias is a real concern. Name the source, the selection procedure, and whether tasks were filtered after seeing OrchestrA's behavior.

**6. "Deployment-ready" is not earned by the evidence shown.**
Nothing in the abstract speaks to latency, cost, failure modes under adversarial inputs, or operational concerns. Drop "deployment-ready" or back it with concrete operational results in the paper.

**7. Minor:**
- "typed message bus" appears in paragraph 1 but "typed interface" / "role-typed message passing" in paragraphs 2–3. Pick one term and use it consistently.
- "binding constraint" is jargon for an economics-flavored claim; in an ML/SE venue, plain phrasing is better.
- The third component ("workspace layer") is described as enabling rollback "to any prior consistent state" — define consistency. Content-addressed storage alone does not give you a consistency model.

## Suggested rewrite of the results paragraph

> We evaluate OrchestrA on 32 software-engineering tasks sampled from SWE-bench-style repositories [cite; describe selection]. Each task requires the agent team to produce a patch that passes the project's existing test suite. OrchestrA completes 24/32 tasks end-to-end versus 17/32 for a single-agent ReAct baseline (McNemar p=…) and 20/32 for an AutoGen-based multi-agent baseline. An ablation that disables workspace checkpointing while keeping roles and the scheduler fixed completes 19/32, isolating ~5 of the additional successes to the checkpointing layer.

(Numbers illustrative; you'd fill in real ones.)

## Suggested rewrite of the closing paragraph

> These results suggest that, on this benchmark, substrate-level mechanisms — particularly rollback over a shared workspace — contribute more to team success than the prompt variations we explored. OrchestrA is structured so that additional role specifications and tool surfaces can be added without changes to the scheduler or workspace contract, which we expect will make it useful as a research substrate for multi-agent SE work.

## Bottom line

The writing is clean and the system description is clear, but the empirical claims outrun the evidence in three places (novelty, causal attribution, generalization), and the experimental design is missing the ablation that would make the central claim defensible. Fix those three before submitting.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the current state of AutoGen/MetaGPT/CrewAI/LangGraph feature sets against your "first to combine" claim — I'm flagging it as a likely review attack, not asserting prior art exists with certainty. Statistical recommendation (McNemar) assumes the two systems were run on the same 32 tasks; if not, use a different test.
